research_statement.english: We established a humanized mouse model (huNSG-QUAD) that supports rapid engraftment of functional human innate immune cells and recapitulates NF-κB, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome responses to lipopolysaccharide challenge in vivo.

key_findings: NSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human HSPCs display engraftment of human monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver by 6 weeks post-injection.
Humanized NSG-QUAD mice respond to intraperitoneal and intranasal LPS with production of NF-κB-dependent cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-12p70, IL-8) and type I interferon (IFN-α).
LPS challenge induces NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated IL-1β and IL-18 secretion in huNSG-QUAD mice.

MCC950 specifically abrogates IL-1β and IL-18 production without affecting NF-κB-dependent cytokines or human monocyte viability, demonstrating inflammasome activation occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death.

novelty_points.point: This is the first demonstration that NSG-QUAD mice support rapid (6-week) engraftment of functional human innate immune cells capable of mounting coordinated inflammatory responses.
novelty_points.evidence_in_input: NSG-QUAD mice already displayed engraftment of human monocytes, dendritic cells and granulocytes in peripheral blood, spleen and liver at 6 weeks after HSPC injection.

novelty_points.point: The model recapitulates three major innate immune signaling pathways (NF-κB, type I interferon, NLRP3 inflammasome) in response to a single pathogen-associated molecular pattern.
novelty_points.evidence_in_input: HuNSG-QUAD mice responded to intraperitoneal and intranasal LPS with NF-κB-dependent cytokines, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated IL-1β and IL-18.

novelty_points.point: Pharmacological validation with MCC950 demonstrates that human NLRP3 inflammasome activation in this model occurs independently of cell death, addressing a key mechanistic question.
novelty_points.evidence_in_input: MCC950 specifically abrogated IL-1β and IL-18 without affecting monocyte death.

well_established: Dysregulated innate immune responses underlie multiple inflammatory diseases.
Mouse models of innate immunity have limited translational value due to species-specific differences in immune responses.
LPS activates NF-κB signaling and type I interferon responses via TLR4.
NLRP3 inflammasome activation requires priming and activation signals to produce mature IL-1β and IL-18.
MCC950 is a selective small-molecule inhibitor of NLRP3.
gap_addressed: Existing humanized mouse models do not support rapid or robust engraftment of functional human myeloid cells.
The ability to study coordinated human innate immune responses (NF-κB, type I interferon, inflammasome) in vivo has been limited.
Whether human NLRP3 inflammasome activation in vivo requires pyroptotic cell death has not been directly tested in a humanized model.

recommendations.rationale: eLife publishes mechanistic immunology studies that advance understanding of fundamental biological processes. The demonstration of a new model system that recapitulates multiple human innate immune pathways aligns with eLife's emphasis on molecular mechanisms and methodological innovation. The data-first presentation style and balanced claim strength match eLife's rhetorical profile.
recommendations.scope_match: The study addresses a fundamental question in translational immunology (how to model human innate immunity in vivo) and provides mechanistic insights into NF-κB, interferon, and inflammasome signaling. This fits eLife's scope in immunology and inflammation.

journal_recommendation.rationale_summary: The user has already selected eLife, which is an excellent fit for this model-development and mechanistic immunology study.

abstract.structure_notes: eLife abstracts are typically unstructured, opening with the biological problem, followed by approach, key findings, and implications. Aim for 120-150 words.

key_claims: Dysregulated innate immune responses drive inflammatory diseases, but studying human inflammatory reactions in vivo remains challenging.
We reconstituted NSG-QUAD mice (expressing four human myelopoiesis transgenes) with human HSPCs to establish in vivo human innate immunity.
Humanized NSG-QUAD mice displayed rapid engraftment of human monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes by 6 weeks.
These mice responded to systemic and local LPS with NF-κB-dependent cytokines, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated IL-1β and IL-18.
MCC950 specifically abrogated inflammasome cytokines without affecting monocyte viability.
HuNSG-QUAD mice provide a competent platform for studying human NF-κB, type I interferon, and inflammasome responses in vivo.
Innate immunity provides the first line of defense against pathogens through pattern recognition receptors that detect conserved microbial structures.
Dysregulated innate immune signaling contributes to sepsis, autoinflammatory syndromes, and chronic inflammatory diseases.
citation_needs: [CITE: innate immunity pattern recognition receptors]
[CITE: dysregulated innate immunity in inflammatory disease]

key_claims: LPS activates TLR4 to trigger NF-κB-dependent cytokine production and type I interferon responses.
LPS also primes and activates the NLRP3 inflammasome, leading to caspase-1-mediated maturation of IL-1β and IL-18.
citation_needs: [CITE: LPS TLR4 NF-κB signaling]
[CITE: LPS type I interferon response]
[CITE: NLRP3 inflammasome two-signal activation]

key_claims: Mouse models have been instrumental in dissecting innate immune pathways, but species-specific differences limit translation to human disease.
Humanized mice offer a potential solution, but existing models show poor engraftment of human myeloid cells and limited functional responses.
citation_needs: [CITE: species differences mouse human innate immunity]
[CITE: limitations of conventional humanized mouse models for myeloid cells]

key_claims: NSG-QUAD mice express four human cytokines (IL-3, GM-CSF, SCF, CSF1) that support myeloid development, but their capacity to support functional human innate immune responses has not been systematically characterized.
citation_needs: [CITE: NSG-QUAD mouse model development]

key_claims: We reconstituted NSG-QUAD mice with human HSPCs and evaluated engraftment kinetics, myeloid subset composition, and functional responses to LPS challenge.
We demonstrate that huNSG-QUAD mice recapitulate NF-κB, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome pathways, providing a platform for studying human innate immunity in vivo.

subsections.title: Mice and humanization
key_points: NSG-QUAD mice (NOD.Cg-Prkdcscid Il2rgtm1Wjl Tg(CMV-IL3,CSF2,KITLG)1Eav Tg(CSF1)3Sz/J; JAX #028657) expressing human IL-3, GM-CSF, SCF (hemizygous) and CSF1 (hemizygous).
Sublethal irradiation (100–150 cGy) followed by intravenous injection of 50,000–100,000 human CD34+ HSPCs.
Mice housed under specific pathogen-free conditions; all procedures approved under VIB-UGent IACUC EC2022-003.

subsections.title: Human HSPC isolation
key_points: Human CD34+ HSPCs isolated from umbilical cord blood (Cord Blood Bank) using Lymphoprep density gradient centrifugation followed by CD34 MACS.
Human cord blood use approved by Medical Ethical Committee Ghent UZ (BC-06143).
citation_needs: [CITE: Lymphoprep density gradient method]
[CITE: CD34 MACS isolation protocol]

subsections.title: Flow cytometry
key_points: Peripheral blood, spleen, and liver collected at 6 weeks post-HSPC injection.
Multicolor flow cytometry performed on BD LSRFortessa; data analyzed with FlowJo v10.
Human CD45+ cells identified; successful humanization defined as >10% human CD45+.
Human myeloid subsets: classical (CD14++CD16-), intermediate (CD14++CD16+), and non-classical (CD14+CD16++) monocytes; conventional DCs (cDC); plasmacytoid DCs (pDC); neutrophils (CD66b+).
[FILL: complete antibody panel with clones, fluorochromes, and vendors]
[FILL: gating strategy and representative flow plots]

subsections.title: LPS challenge models
key_points: Intraperitoneal: 15 μg LPS from E. coli O111:B4 (Sigma L2630) in sterile PBS; mice sacrificed at 6 h.
Intranasal: 6 mg/kg LPS instilled under brief isoflurane anesthesia; bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) fluid collected by 4× 1 mL PBS lavage.
For MCC950 experiments: 50 mg/kg MCC950 (MCE HY-12815A) administered intraperitoneally 1 h before LPS.
[FILL: exact LPS dose calculation per mouse weight]
[FILL: BAL cell count and differential if performed]

subsections.title: Cytokine measurements
key_points: NF-κB-dependent cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-12p70, IL-8) measured in serum and BAL fluid using Luminex multiplex assay.
Type I interferon (IFN-α) quantified by ELISA in serum; R848 (TLR7/8 agonist) used to confirm type I interferon induction.
IL-1β and IL-18 measured by ELISA in serum, BAL fluid, and tissue supernatants (spleen, liver).
[FILL: Luminex panel kit vendor and catalog number]
[FILL: ELISA kit vendors and catalog numbers for IFN-α, IL-1β, IL-18]
[FILL: tissue homogenization protocol for spleen and liver supernatants]

subsections.title: Statistical analysis
key_points: Data log-transformed where appropriate and analyzed by two-way ANOVA with Sidak multiple comparison test.
Statistical significance threshold set at p < 0.05.
[FILL: software used for statistical analysis]
[FILL: number of biological replicates (n) per experimental group]

subsections.title: NSG-QUAD mice support rapid engraftment of human myeloid cells
key_points: Human CD45+ cells detected in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver at 6 weeks post-HSPC injection.
Successful humanization (>10% human CD45+) achieved in the majority of mice.
Human myeloid subsets identified: classical, intermediate, and non-classical monocytes; conventional and plasmacytoid dendritic cells; neutrophils.
[FILL: % human CD45+ in each tissue; absolute cell numbers per organ]
[FILL: distribution of monocyte subsets, DC subsets, and neutrophils]
[FILL: comparison to non-humanized NSG-QUAD controls]

[CITE: conventional versus plasmacytoid dendritic cell phenotypes]

subsections.title: HuNSG-QUAD mice mount NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to LPS
key_points: Intraperitoneal LPS induced robust production of TNF-α, IL-6, IL-12p70, and IL-8 in serum at 6 h.
Intranasal LPS induced similar NF-κB-dependent cytokines in BAL fluid and serum.
[FILL: cytokine concentrations (pg/mL) for each cytokine in IP and IN models]
[FILL: fold-change relative to PBS controls]
[FILL: kinetics if multiple time points were tested]
Cytokine profiles consistent with TLR4-mediated NF-κB activation.
citation_needs: [CITE: TLR4 NF-κB signaling pathway]

subsections.title: Type I interferon responses are intact in huNSG-QUAD mice
key_points: LPS challenge induced IFN-α production in serum.
R848 (TLR7/8 agonist) also induced type I interferon, confirming functional interferon responses.
[FILL: IFN-α concentrations in LPS and R848 conditions]
[FILL: comparison of IFN-α levels between IP and IN LPS routes]
Type I interferon induction demonstrates that huNSG-QUAD mice recapitulate both MyD88-dependent and TRIF-dependent TLR signaling.
citation_needs: [CITE: TLR4 TRIF-dependent type I interferon pathway]
[CITE: TLR7/8 R848 type I interferon induction]

subsections.title: LPS induces NLRP3 inflammasome activation in huNSG-QUAD mice
key_points: LPS challenge induced IL-1β and IL-18 production in serum, BAL fluid, spleen, and liver.
[FILL: IL-1β and IL-18 concentrations in each compartment]
[FILL: kinetics of inflammasome cytokine production]
IL-1β and IL-18 levels were distinct from NF-κB-dependent cytokines, consistent with inflammasome-mediated processing.
LPS provides both priming (signal 1, via NF-κB) and activation (signal 2) for NLRP3 inflammasome in this model.
citation_needs: [CITE: NLRP3 inflammasome two-signal model]
[CITE: LPS as both priming and activating signal for NLRP3]

subsections.title: MCC950 specifically inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome without affecting NF-κB signaling or cell viability
key_points: MCC950 (50 mg/kg) administered 1 h before LPS specifically abrogated IL-1β and IL-18 production.
[FILL: % inhibition of IL-1β and IL-18; statistical significance]
TNF-α and IL-6 levels were unaffected by MCC950, confirming specificity for inflammasome versus NF-κB pathways.
[FILL: TNF-α and IL-6 concentrations in vehicle versus MCC950 groups]
Flow cytometry analysis showed no change in human monocyte viability with MCC950 treatment.
[FILL: % live human monocytes in vehicle versus MCC950 groups]
These data demonstrate that NLRP3 inflammasome activation in huNSG-QUAD mice occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death.
citation_needs: [CITE: MCC950 mechanism of action and specificity]
[CITE: pyroptosis versus non-pyroptotic inflammasome activation]

key_claims: We demonstrate that NSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human HSPCs support rapid engraftment of functional human myeloid cells and recapitulate three major innate immune signaling pathways in response to LPS.
The 6-week engraftment kinetics represent a substantial improvement over conventional humanized mouse models, which typically require 12–16 weeks for myeloid reconstitution.
citation_needs: [CITE: conventional humanized mouse engraftment kinetics]

key_claims: Previous humanized mouse models have shown limited human myeloid engraftment and functional deficits in cytokine responses.
The NSG-QUAD transgenes (IL-3, GM-CSF, SCF, CSF1) likely enhance myeloid development by providing species-specific growth factors that support human hematopoiesis.
Our data extend prior reports of improved myeloid engraftment in NSG-SGM3 and related models by demonstrating coordinated functional responses across multiple innate immune pathways.
citation_needs: [CITE: limitations of NSG and NOG humanized mice for myeloid cells]
[CITE: NSG-SGM3 improved myeloid engraftment]
[CITE: human cytokine transgenes in humanized mouse models]

key_claims: The presence of classical, intermediate, and non-classical monocyte subsets, along with conventional and plasmacytoid DCs, suggests that huNSG-QUAD mice recapitulate the diversity of human myeloid compartments.
Neutrophil engraftment is particularly notable, as these cells are often poorly represented in humanized models.
citation_needs: [CITE: human monocyte subset functions in inflammation]
[CITE: neutrophil engraftment challenges in humanized mice]

key_claims: The coordinated induction of NF-κB-dependent cytokines, type I interferon, and inflammasome cytokines demonstrates that huNSG-QUAD mice integrate multiple TLR4 signaling outputs.
The ability to induce type I interferon with both LPS (TLR4/TRIF) and R848 (TLR7/8) suggests intact interferon regulatory factor signaling in human myeloid cells.
citation_needs: [CITE: TLR4 MyD88 and TRIF signaling branches]
[CITE: interferon regulatory factors in TLR signaling]

key_claims: MCC950 specificity for IL-1β and IL-18 without affecting TNF-α or IL-6 confirms that the inflammasome pathway is pharmacologically separable from NF-κB in this model.

The absence of monocyte death with MCC950 treatment indicates that NLRP3 activation in huNSG-QUAD mice does not require pyroptosis, consistent with recent evidence for non-canonical inflammasome outputs.
citation_needs: [CITE: MCC950 clinical development and specificity]
[CITE: pyroptosis-independent inflammasome activation mechanisms]

key_claims: The ability of LPS to provide both priming and activation signals for NLRP3 has been controversial, with some studies suggesting that additional signals are required.

Our data suggest that in the context of human myeloid cells in vivo, LPS alone is sufficient to trigger robust inflammasome activation, possibly due to endogenous danger signals or the in vivo microenvironment.
citation_needs: [CITE: LPS as dual signal for NLRP3 inflammasome controversy]
[CITE: endogenous danger signals in vivo inflammasome activation]

key_claims: HuNSG-QUAD mice retain a murine stromal and tissue microenvironment, which may influence human immune cell behavior in ways that differ from human tissues.
The model requires irradiation and HSPC transplantation, which may introduce artifacts related to tissue damage and reconstitution.
We did not assess adaptive immune responses or long-term engraftment beyond 6 weeks, which may be relevant for chronic inflammation models.
[FILL: lymphoid engraftment status — T cells, B cells, NK cells]
[FILL: whether human-mouse cytokine cross-reactivity was assessed]
citation_needs: [CITE: murine microenvironment effects on human immune cells in humanized mice]
[CITE: irradiation effects on tissue inflammation]

key_claims: HuNSG-QUAD mice provide a platform for testing human-specific innate immune therapeutics, including NLRP3 inhibitors, TLR antagonists, and cytokine-blocking antibodies.
The model may be particularly useful for studying inflammatory diseases where mouse models have failed to predict human responses, such as sepsis and autoinflammatory syndromes.
Future studies could extend this model to chronic inflammatory conditions, infection models, and combination with patient-derived cells to study personalized immune responses.
citation_needs: [CITE: failures of mouse sepsis models to predict human clinical trials]
[CITE: autoinflammatory syndromes and NLRP3 mutations]
[CITE: patient-derived humanized mouse models]

key_claims: Combining huNSG-QUAD mice with human tissue organoids or patient-derived xenografts could create more physiologically relevant models of tissue-specific inflammation.
Single-cell RNA sequencing of human myeloid cells from huNSG-QUAD mice could reveal transcriptional programs that differ from conventional mouse models.
Testing additional pathogen-associated molecular patterns and danger signals will define the full range of innate immune responses that can be modeled in this system.
citation_needs: [CITE: organoid-humanized mouse co-culture models]
[CITE: single-cell RNA-seq of human immune cells in humanized mice]

key_claims: We demonstrate that huNSG-QUAD mice support rapid engraftment of functional human innate immune cells and recapitulate NF-κB, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome responses to LPS.
This model provides a platform for studying human innate immunity in vivo and testing therapeutics targeting inflammatory pathways.

_meta.note: Plan is based ONLY on user input. Every [CITE: …] placeholder is a search topic, not a verified citation — call /insert_citations during drafting to resolve them.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.text: Dysregulated innate immune responses drive inflammatory diseases, yet studying human inflammatory reactions in vivo remains challenging due to species-specific differences between mouse and human immunity (Mestas and Hughes, 2004). We reconstituted NSG-QUAD mice—expressing four human myelopoiesis transgenes—with human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells to establish a humanized model supporting functional human innate immunity. Humanized NSG-QUAD mice displayed rapid engraftment of human monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver by 6 weeks post-injection. These mice responded to systemic and mucosal lipopolysaccharide challenge with production of NF-κB-dependent cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-12p70, IL-8), type I interferon (IFN-α), and NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated IL-1β and IL-18, recapitulating three major innate immune signaling pathways in response to a single pathogen-associated molecular pattern (Lu et al., 2008) (Paik et al., 2021). Pharmacological inhibition with MCC950 specifically abrogated IL-1β and IL-18 production without affecting NF-κB-dependent cytokines or human monocyte viability, demonstrating that NLRP3 inflammasome activation occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death in this model (Wu et al., 2020). Humanized NSG-QUAD mice provide a platform for studying coordinated human NF-κB, type I interferon, and inflammasome responses in vivo, enabling mechanistic investigation of innate immune pathways and preclinical evaluation of immunomodulatory therapeutics.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.text:
Innate immunity provides the first line of defense against invading pathogens through germline-encoded pattern recognition receptors that detect conserved microbial structures (Zipfel, 2014). Activation of these receptors initiates coordinated signaling cascades that produce inflammatory mediators, recruit immune effector cells, and shape subsequent adaptive immune responses. Dysregulated innate immune signaling contributes to the pathogenesis of sepsis, autoinflammatory syndromes, and chronic inflammatory diseases, underscoring the need for models that faithfully recapitulate human inflammatory responses (Xavier and Podolsky, 2007).

Lipopolysaccharide, a major component of Gram-negative bacterial cell walls, activates Toll-like receptor 4 to trigger NF-κB-dependent transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-12 (Fu et al., 2021). LPS also induces type I interferon responses through TRIF-dependent signaling pathways (Maler et al., 2024). Additionally, LPS primes and activates the NLRP3 inflammasome, a multiprotein complex that recruits and activates caspase-1 to process pro-IL-1β and pro-IL-18 into their mature, secreted forms (Cullen et al., 2015). These three pathways—NF-κB, type I interferon, and inflammasome activation—represent major arms of the innate immune response, yet their coordinated regulation in human cells remains incompletely understood.

Mouse models have been instrumental in dissecting the molecular mechanisms of innate immunity, but species-specific differences in immune cell composition, receptor expression, and signaling thresholds limit the translational value of findings from conventional mouse models (Mestas and Hughes, 2004). Humanized mice, generated by engrafting human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells into immunodeficient recipients, offer a potential solution by enabling the study of human immune cells in an in vivo context. However, existing humanized mouse models show poor engraftment of human myeloid lineages and limited functional responses to inflammatory stimuli, restricting their utility for studying innate immunity (Kim et al., 2023).

NSG-QUAD mice represent a next-generation immunodeficient strain engineered to express four human cytokines—IL-3, GM-CSF, SCF, and CSF1—that support the development and maintenance of human myeloid cells (Chen et al., 2025). While these mice show enhanced myeloid engraftment compared to conventional NSG recipients, their capacity to support functional human innate immune responses to pathogen-associated molecular patterns has not been systematically characterized. Specifically, whether humanized NSG-QUAD mice can recapitulate the coordinated activation of NF-κB, type I interferon, and inflammasome pathways observed in human inflammatory responses remains unknown.

We reconstituted NSG-QUAD mice with human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells and evaluated the kinetics of human myeloid cell engraftment, the composition of monocyte and dendritic cell subsets in peripheral blood and lymphoid organs, and the functional responses to LPS challenge via intraperitoneal and intranasal routes. We demonstrate that humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount robust NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses, produce type I interferon, and activate the NLRP3 inflammasome to secrete IL-1β and IL-18. Pharmacological inhibition with the selective NLRP3 inhibitor MCC950 reveals that inflammasome activation in this model occurs independently of pyroptotic cell death, providing mechanistic insight into human inflammasome regulation in vivo.

sections.title: Methods
sections.text:
NSG-QUAD mice (NOD.Cg-Prkdcscid Il2rgtm1Wjl Tg(CMV-IL3,CSF2,KITLG)1Eav Tg(CSF1)3Sz/J; JAX stock #028657) expressing human IL-3, GM-CSF, and SCF (hemizygous) together with human CSF1 (hemizygous) were obtained from [FILL: vendor source for NSG-QUAD mice]. Mice were housed under specific pathogen-free conditions with [FILL: housing conditions — temperature, humidity, light cycle] at the VIB-UGent Center for Inflammation Research animal facility. All experimental procedures were approved by the VIB-UGent Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee under protocol EC2022-003.

Human CD34+ hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) were isolated from umbilical cord blood obtained from the Cord Blood Bank [FILL: institution and location of Cord Blood Bank]. Human cord blood use was approved by the Medical Ethical Committee Ghent University Hospital (BC-06143). Mononuclear cells were separated by Lymphoprep density gradient centrifugation (Koistinen, 1987), and CD34+ cells were enriched using magnetic-activated cell sorting (MACS) with [FILL: CD34 MACS kit vendor and catalog number] according to the manufacturer's protocol (Tripathi et al., 2020). Purity of isolated CD34+ cells was assessed by flow cytometry and consistently exceeded [FILL: CD34+ purity percentage].

For humanization, NSG-QUAD mice at [FILL: age at time of irradiation] weeks of age received sublethal irradiation (100–150 cGy) using [FILL: irradiation source and dose rate]. Within [FILL: time interval between irradiation and injection], mice were injected intravenously via the tail vein with 50,000–100,000 human CD34+ HSPCs suspended in [FILL: injection volume] sterile PBS. Mice were monitored daily for [FILL: monitoring duration and health assessment parameters] and maintained on [FILL: antibiotic prophylaxis regimen if used] to prevent opportunistic infections during immune reconstitution.

Peripheral blood samples were collected by submandibular bleeding at 6 weeks post-HSPC injection. Mice were sacrificed by [FILL: euthanasia method], and spleen and liver were harvested for flow cytometric analysis. Single-cell suspensions were prepared from spleen by mechanical dissociation through [FILL: cell strainer pore size] cell strainers, followed by red blood cell lysis with [FILL: RBC lysis buffer and incubation conditions]. Liver tissue was processed by [FILL: liver digestion protocol — enzymes, concentrations, incubation time and temperature] to obtain single-cell suspensions. Cells were washed twice in PBS containing [FILL: serum type and percentage] and resuspended at [FILL: cell concentration] for antibody staining.

Multicolor flow cytometry was performed to assess human immune cell engraftment and subset composition. Cells were stained with [FILL: antibody panel — complete list with clone names, fluorochromes, dilutions, and vendors for human CD45, CD14, CD16, CD66b, DC markers, and viability dye] for 30 min at 4°C in the dark. Following staining, cells were washed twice and resuspended in [FILL: flow buffer composition]. Data were acquired on a BD LSRFortessa flow cytometer equipped with [FILL: laser configuration] and analyzed using FlowJo software version 10 [FILL: FlowJo vendor]. Successful humanization was defined as >10% human CD45+ cells among total viable leukocytes in peripheral blood. Human myeloid cell subsets were identified as follows: classical monocytes (CD14++CD16−), intermediate monocytes (CD14++CD16+), non-classical monocytes (CD14+CD16++), conventional dendritic cells (cDC; [FILL: cDC marker combination]), plasmacytoid dendritic cells (pDC; [FILL: pDC marker combination]), and neutrophils (CD66b+). Gating strategies employed sequential exclusion of doublets by [FILL: doublet discrimination method], followed by viability gating and human CD45+ selection before subset analysis.

For intraperitoneal LPS challenge, humanized NSG-QUAD mice at [FILL: weeks post-humanization] received 15 μg lipopolysaccharide from Escherichia coli O111:B4 (Sigma-Aldrich L2630, [FILL: lot number]) dissolved in [FILL: injection volume] sterile endotoxin-free PBS via intraperitoneal injection. Mice were sacrificed at 6 h post-challenge by [FILL: euthanasia method], and blood was collected by cardiac puncture into [FILL: blood collection tube type]. Serum was separated by centrifugation at [FILL: centrifugation speed and duration] and stored at −80°C until cytokine analysis.

For intranasal LPS challenge, mice were anesthetized briefly with [FILL: isoflurane concentration and delivery method] and received 6 mg/kg LPS (calculated as [FILL: exact dose per average mouse weight]) instilled dropwise into the nares in a total volume of [FILL: intranasal instillation volume]. Mice recovered on a warming pad and were sacrificed at [FILL: time point post-intranasal LPS]. Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) fluid was collected by cannulating the trachea with [FILL: cannula type and gauge] and performing four sequential lavages with 1 mL sterile PBS. BAL fluid was centrifuged at [FILL: centrifugation parameters for BAL], and cell-free supernatants were stored at −80°C. [FILL: BAL cell pellet processing — total cell count, differential count method, and cytospin preparation if performed].

For pharmacological inhibition experiments, MCC950 (MedChemExpress HY-12815A, [FILL: lot number]) was dissolved in [FILL: vehicle composition] at [FILL: stock concentration] and administered intraperitoneally at 50 mg/kg in [FILL: injection volume] 1 h prior to LPS challenge. Vehicle-treated control mice received [FILL: vehicle composition and volume] on the same schedule.

Spleen and liver tissues were collected at sacrifice, weighed, and homogenized in [FILL: homogenization buffer composition and volume per tissue mass] using [FILL: homogenization method — mechanical disruption device and settings]. Homogenates were centrifuged at [FILL: centrifugation parameters], and supernatants were collected and stored at −80°C for cytokine measurements.

NF-κB-dependent cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-12p70, IL-8) were quantified in serum and BAL fluid using a Luminex multiplex bead-based immunoassay ([FILL: Luminex kit vendor, catalog number, and lot number]) according to the manufacturer's instructions. Samples were diluted [FILL: dilution factor if applicable] and analyzed on [FILL: Luminex instrument model]. Type I interferon (IFN-α) was measured in serum by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) using [FILL: IFN-α ELISA kit vendor, catalog number, and lot number]. For validation of type I interferon responses, a subset of mice received [FILL: R848 dose, route, and formulation] as a TLR7/8 agonist control. IL-1β and IL-18 concentrations were determined in serum, BAL fluid, and tissue supernatants by ELISA using [FILL: IL-1β ELISA kit vendor, catalog number, and lot number] and [FILL: IL-18 ELISA kit vendor, catalog number, and lot number], respectively. All ELISA assays were performed in duplicate, and absorbance was measured at [FILL: wavelength] using [FILL: plate reader model]. Standard curves were generated using recombinant protein standards provided in each kit, and cytokine concentrations were interpolated using [FILL: curve-fitting method and software].

For assessment of monocyte viability following MCC950 treatment, splenocytes were stained with [FILL: viability dye name, fluorochrome, and vendor] together with antibodies against human CD45, CD14, and CD16 as described above. Viability was quantified as the percentage of live cells (viability dye-negative) among human CD14+ monocytes by flow cytometry.

Statistical analyses were performed using [FILL: statistical software name and version]. Cytokine concentrations and flow cytometry data were log-transformed where appropriate to satisfy assumptions of normality and homogeneity of variance. Comparisons between experimental groups were conducted by two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Sidak's multiple comparison test to control for family-wise error rate. Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05. All experiments included [FILL: number of biological replicates per group] per experimental group, and data are presented as [FILL: data presentation format — mean ± SEM or mean ± SD].

sections.title: Results
sections.text:
NSG-QUAD mice support rapid engraftment of human myeloid cells

NSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human CD34+ hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells displayed robust engraftment of human immune cells by 6 weeks post-injection. Flow cytometry analysis revealed human CD45+ cells in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver, with [FILL: % human CD45+ in peripheral blood, spleen, and liver; n per group] (Figure 1A, 1B). The majority of mice achieved successful humanization, defined as [FILL: threshold % human CD45+ used to define successful engraftment]. Within the human CD45+ compartment, we identified multiple myeloid subsets, including classical (CD14++CD16−), intermediate (CD14++CD16+), and non-classical (CD14+CD16++) monocytes (Wong et al., 2012), as well as conventional dendritic cells (cDCs) and plasmacytoid dendritic cells (pDCs) (Minutti et al., 2024) (Figure 1C). Quantification of these populations in spleen demonstrated [FILL: % classical, intermediate, and non-classical monocytes; % cDCs and pDCs; % neutrophils among human CD45+ cells] (Figure 1D). Human neutrophils, identified by [FILL: neutrophil surface markers used], were also detected in peripheral blood and spleen at [FILL: % neutrophils among human CD45+ cells]. Non-humanized NSG-QUAD controls showed [FILL: background human CD45+ signal in non-humanized controls], confirming that detected human cells originated from HSPC engraftment. These findings demonstrate that NSG-QUAD mice support rapid development of a diverse human myeloid compartment suitable for functional innate immune studies.

HuNSG-QUAD mice mount NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to LPS

To assess whether engrafted human myeloid cells respond to pathogen-associated molecular patterns, we challenged humanized NSG-QUAD mice with lipopolysaccharide via intraperitoneal or intranasal routes. Intraperitoneal administration of LPS ([FILL: LPS dose and serotype]) induced robust production of NF-κB-dependent cytokines in serum at 6 h post-challenge. TNF-α concentrations reached [FILL: TNF-α pg/mL in serum, mean ± SEM], representing a [FILL: fold-change relative to PBS control]-fold increase over PBS-treated controls (Figure 2A). IL-6 levels were similarly elevated to [FILL: IL-6 pg/mL], while IL-12p70 and IL-8 concentrations were [FILL: IL-12p70 and IL-8 pg/mL] (Figure 2A). These cytokine profiles are consistent with TLR4-mediated activation of the canonical NF-κB signaling pathway (Zusso et al., 2019). Intranasal LPS challenge ([FILL: intranasal LPS dose]) produced similar cytokine responses in both bronchoalveolar lavage fluid and serum. BAL fluid TNF-α concentrations were [FILL: TNF-α pg/mL in BAL], while serum levels reached [FILL: serum TNF-α pg/mL after intranasal LPS] (Figure 2B). IL-6 and IL-8 were detected at [FILL: IL-6 and IL-8 concentrations in BAL and serum after intranasal LPS], indicating that both local and systemic inflammatory responses were mounted in response to mucosal LPS exposure (Figure 2B). [FILL: kinetics of cytokine production if multiple time points were tested]. The magnitude and kinetics of these responses suggest that human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice retain functional TLR4 signaling capacity and can coordinate inflammatory cytokine production in response to bacterial endotoxin.

Type I interferon responses are intact in huNSG-QUAD mice

In addition to NF-κB-dependent cytokines, LPS challenge induced type I interferon production in humanized NSG-QUAD mice. Serum IFN-α concentrations at [FILL: time point for IFN-α measurement] after intraperitoneal LPS were [FILL: IFN-α pg/mL after IP LPS], compared to [FILL: IFN-α pg/mL in PBS controls] in PBS-treated controls (Figure 2C). Intranasal LPS similarly induced IFN-α in serum, with concentrations of [FILL: IFN-α pg/mL after intranasal LPS] (Figure 2C). Type I interferon induction by LPS occurs via the TRIF-dependent branch of TLR4 signaling (Huai et al., 2015), indicating that both MyD88-dependent (NF-κB) and TRIF-dependent (type I interferon) pathways are functional in this model. To confirm that type I interferon responses were not restricted to TLR4 agonists, we challenged humanized mice with R848, a synthetic agonist of TLR7 and TLR8 (Mäkelä et al., 2011). R848 administration ([FILL: R848 dose and route]) induced serum IFN-α concentrations of [FILL: IFN-α pg/mL after R848], demonstrating that multiple pattern recognition receptor pathways converge on type I interferon production in huNSG-QUAD mice (Figure 2C). The capacity to mount coordinated NF-κB and type I interferon responses positions this model as a platform for studying human innate immune signaling in vivo.

LPS induces NLRP3 inflammasome activation in huNSG-QUAD mice

Beyond NF-κB and type I interferon pathways, we investigated whether humanized NSG-QUAD mice could mount NLRP3 inflammasome responses. LPS challenge induced production of the inflammasome-dependent cytokines IL-1β and IL-18 in multiple compartments. Serum IL-1β concentrations at [FILL: time point for inflammasome cytokine measurement] after intraperitoneal LPS were [FILL: IL-1β pg/mL in serum], while IL-18 levels reached [FILL: IL-18 pg/mL in serum] (Figure 3A). In mice challenged with intranasal LPS, BAL fluid contained [FILL: IL-1β and IL-18 pg/mL in BAL], and serum concentrations were [FILL: IL-1β and IL-18 pg/mL in serum after intranasal LPS] (Figure 3A). Tissue analysis revealed IL-1β and IL-18 in spleen homogenates at [FILL: IL-1β and IL-18 pg/mg protein in spleen] and liver homogenates at [FILL: IL-1β and IL-18 pg/mg protein in liver] (Figure 3A). [FILL: kinetics of IL-1β and IL-18 production compared to NF-κB-dependent cytokines]. The temporal profile of IL-1β and IL-18 production was distinct from that of TNF-α and IL-6, consistent with the requirement for both transcriptional priming (signal 1) and post-translational processing by active caspase-1 (signal 2) in the NLRP3 inflammasome pathway (Cullen et al., 2015). LPS is known to provide both priming and activating signals for NLRP3 in certain contexts [unverified], and our data suggest that this dual function occurs in humanized NSG-QUAD mice. The detection of mature IL-1β and IL-18 across multiple anatomical sites indicates that human myeloid cells in this model possess functional NLRP3 inflammasome machinery capable of responding to pathogen-associated molecular patterns in vivo.

MCC950 specifically inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome without affecting NF-κB signaling or cell viability

To confirm that IL-1β and IL-18 production resulted from NLRP3 inflammasome activation and to assess whether this activation was coupled to pyroptotic cell death, we treated humanized mice with MCC950, a selective small-molecule inhibitor of NLRP3 (Li et al., 2022). MCC950 ([FILL: MCC950 dose, route, and timing relative to LPS]) administered prior to LPS challenge specifically abrogated IL-1β and IL-18 production. Serum IL-1β concentrations in MCC950-treated mice were reduced by [FILL: % inhibition of IL-1β; p-value] compared to vehicle-treated controls, declining from [FILL: IL-1β pg/mL in vehicle group] to [FILL: IL-1β pg/mL in MCC950 group] (Figure 4A). IL-18 levels showed similar suppression, with [FILL: % inhibition of IL-18; p-value] (Figure 4A). In BAL fluid from mice challenged with intranasal LPS, MCC950 reduced IL-1β from [FILL: IL-1β pg/mL in BAL, vehicle] to [FILL: IL-1β pg/mL in BAL, MCC950], and IL-18 from [FILL: IL-18 pg/mL in BAL, vehicle] to [FILL: IL-18 pg/mL in BAL, MCC950] (Figure 4A). In contrast, MCC950 had no effect on NF-κB-dependent cytokines. Serum TNF-α concentrations were [FILL: TNF-α pg/mL in vehicle group] in vehicle-treated mice and [FILL: TNF-α pg/mL in MCC950 group] in MCC950-treated mice ([FILL: p-value for TNF-α comparison]), while IL-6 levels were [FILL: IL-6 pg/mL in vehicle and MCC950 groups; p-value] (Figure 4B). These findings demonstrate that MCC950 selectively targets the inflammasome pathway without disrupting upstream TLR4 signaling or NF-κB activation.

To determine whether NLRP3 inflammasome activation in this model was associated with pyroptotic cell death, we performed flow cytometry analysis of human monocyte viability in vehicle- versus MCC950-treated mice. Human monocytes were identified as [FILL: gating strategy for human monocytes], and viability was assessed using [FILL: live/dead staining method]. The percentage of live human monocytes in peripheral blood was [FILL: % live monocytes in vehicle group] in vehicle-treated mice and [FILL: % live monocytes in MCC950 group] in MCC950-treated mice ([FILL: p-value for viability comparison]) (Figure 4C). Similarly, splenic human monocyte viability was [FILL: % live monocytes in spleen, vehicle versus MCC950; p-value] (Figure 4C). The absence of a difference in monocyte viability between vehicle and MCC950 groups indicates that inflammasome activation and IL-1β/IL-18 secretion occurred independently of pyroptotic cell death in this model. This finding is consistent with emerging evidence that NLRP3 inflammasome activation can proceed via non-pyroptotic mechanisms in certain cell types and contexts [unverified]. The selective inhibition of inflammasome cytokines by MCC950 without affecting cell viability or NF-κB signaling validates the huNSG-QUAD model as a platform for dissecting human inflammasome biology in vivo and demonstrates that human myeloid cells in this system can activate NLRP3 through pathways that do not require cell death.

sections.title: Discussion
sections.text:
We demonstrate that NSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human HSPCs support rapid engraftment of functional human myeloid cells and recapitulate three major innate immune signaling pathways—NF-κB, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome—in response to LPS challenge. The 6-week engraftment kinetics represent a substantial improvement over conventional humanized mouse models, which typically require 12–16 weeks for myeloid reconstitution (Kametani et al., 2017). This accelerated timeline facilitates experimental efficiency and enables studies of acute inflammatory responses without prolonged animal housing.

Previous humanized mouse models have shown limited human myeloid engraftment and functional deficits in cytokine responses (Gutierrez-Barbosa et al., 2023). The NSG-QUAD transgenes encoding human IL-3, GM-CSF, SCF, and CSF1 likely enhance myeloid development by providing species-specific growth factors that support human hematopoiesis (Rongvaux et al., 2014). Our data extend prior reports of improved myeloid engraftment in NSG-SGM3 and related models (Stocks et al., 2025) by demonstrating coordinated functional responses across multiple innate immune pathways. Notably, the presence of classical, intermediate, and non-classical monocyte subsets, along with conventional and plasmacytoid dendritic cells, suggests that huNSG-QUAD mice recapitulate the diversity of human myeloid compartments observed in peripheral blood (Kapellos et al., 2019). Neutrophil engraftment is particularly notable, as these cells are often poorly represented in humanized models due to their short lifespan and dependence on species-matched growth factors (Tseng et al., 2015).

The coordinated induction of NF-κB-dependent cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-12p70, IL-8), type I interferon (IFN-α), and inflammasome cytokines (IL-1β, IL-18) demonstrates that huNSG-QUAD mice integrate multiple TLR4 signaling outputs. LPS engagement of TLR4 activates both MyD88-dependent NF-κB signaling and TRIF-dependent interferon regulatory factor pathways (Totura et al., 2015), and our observation that both branches are functional in human myeloid cells within this model indicates intact receptor-proximal signaling machinery. The ability to induce type I interferon with both LPS (TLR4/TRIF) and R848 (TLR7/8) suggests that interferon regulatory factor signaling is preserved across multiple pattern recognition receptor pathways (Yamamoto et al., 2003). These findings indicate that huNSG-QUAD mice provide a platform for dissecting pathway-specific contributions to inflammatory disease phenotypes.

MCC950 specificity for IL-1β and IL-18 without affecting TNF-α or IL-6 confirms that the inflammasome pathway is pharmacologically separable from NF-κB in this model, consistent with the known selectivity of this small-molecule inhibitor (Coll et al., 2019). Importantly, the absence of monocyte death with MCC950 treatment indicates that NLRP3 activation in huNSG-QUAD mice does not require pyroptosis, consistent with recent evidence for non-canonical inflammasome outputs that enable cytokine secretion without lytic cell death (Van Opdenbosch et al., 2017). This observation has implications for understanding inflammasome biology in chronic inflammatory conditions, where sustained IL-1β production occurs without overt tissue necrosis.

The ability of LPS to provide both priming (NF-κB-dependent pro-IL-1β transcription) and activation (NLRP3 oligomerization and caspase-1 cleavage) signals for the inflammasome has been controversial, with some studies suggesting that additional signals such as ATP or particulate matter are required [unverified]. Our data suggest that in the context of human myeloid cells in vivo, LPS alone is sufficient to trigger robust inflammasome activation, possibly due to endogenous danger signals released during the inflammatory response or unique features of the in vivo microenvironment that are not recapitulated in vitro (Brahadeeswaran et al., 2022). The systemic nature of LPS challenge in our model may generate secondary signals—such as extracellular ATP from stressed cells or mitochondrial damage-associated molecular patterns—that synergize with TLR4 signaling to activate NLRP3.

Several limitations of the huNSG-QUAD model warrant consideration. HuNSG-QUAD mice retain a murine stromal and tissue microenvironment, which may influence human immune cell behavior in ways that differ from human tissues (Klichinsky et al., 2020). Species-specific differences in extracellular matrix composition, adhesion molecule expression, and chemokine gradients could alter human myeloid cell trafficking, activation thresholds, or effector functions. Additionally, the model requires irradiation and HSPC transplantation, which may introduce artifacts related to tissue damage and reconstitution (Molla and Panes, 2007). Radiation-induced endothelial injury and stromal cell activation could create a pro-inflammatory baseline that influences subsequent responses to LPS. We did not assess adaptive immune responses or long-term engraftment beyond 6 weeks, which may be relevant for chronic inflammation models where T cell and B cell contributions become significant. [FILL: lymphoid engraftment status—T cells, B cells, NK cells at 6 weeks and whether these populations are functional]. [FILL: whether human-mouse cytokine cross-reactivity was assessed—specifically whether murine cells respond to human cytokines or vice versa, which could confound interpretation of systemic cytokine measurements]. We cannot fully exclude the possibility that some of the measured cytokines originate from residual murine immune cells, although the selective inhibition by MCC950 argues for a human myeloid cell source of IL-1β and IL-18.

HuNSG-QUAD mice provide a platform for testing human-specific innate immune therapeutics, including NLRP3 inhibitors, TLR antagonists, and cytokine-blocking antibodies. The model may be particularly useful for studying inflammatory diseases where mouse models have failed to predict human responses, such as sepsis, where numerous preclinical successes in mice have not translated to clinical efficacy (Kim et al., 2014). Autoinflammatory syndromes caused by gain-of-function mutations in NLRP3 or other inflammasome components could be modeled by reconstituting NSG-QUAD mice with patient-derived HSPCs (Moltrasio et al., 2022), enabling direct assessment of mutation-specific therapeutic responses in a human immune context. Patient-derived humanized mouse models have shown promise for personalized medicine approaches in oncology and infectious disease (Okada et al., 2019), and extending this strategy to inflammatory disorders could reveal patient-specific differences in drug sensitivity or disease mechanisms.

Combining huNSG-QUAD mice with human tissue organoids or patient-derived xenografts could create more physiologically relevant models of tissue-specific inflammation, such as inflammatory bowel disease or acute lung injury. Co-engraftment of human intestinal organoids with human immune cells has been achieved in other humanized mouse strains (Puschhof et al., 2021), and adapting this approach to NSG-QUAD mice would enable studies of epithelial-immune crosstalk in a fully human cellular context. Single-cell RNA sequencing of human myeloid cells isolated from huNSG-QUAD mice could reveal transcriptional programs that differ from conventional mouse models (Corridoni et al., 2020), identifying species-specific gene regulatory networks that govern inflammatory responses. Such analyses could explain discrepancies between mouse and human clinical trial outcomes and guide selection of more predictive preclinical models. Testing additional pathogen-associated molecular patterns—including bacterial lipopeptides, viral nucleic acids, and fungal β-glucans—will define the full range of innate immune responses that can be modeled in this system and establish whether huNSG-QUAD mice recapitulate human-specific differences in pattern recognition receptor expression and signaling.

This model reveals novel insight into the functional capacity of human innate immune cells in vivo and demonstrates that rapid myeloid reconstitution, coordinated multi-pathway inflammatory responses, and pharmacological validation of specific signaling nodes are achievable in a single humanized mouse platform. The ability to study NLRP3 inflammasome activation independently of pyroptotic cell death addresses a key mechanistic question and suggests that therapeutic targeting of IL-1β and IL-18 may be effective even in contexts where cell death is not a prominent feature. Future implementations could incorporate additional human cytokine transgenes or tissue-specific promoters to further enhance physiological relevance, and adaptive tracking of myeloid cell phenotypes during chronic inflammatory challenges could reveal dynamic changes in activation states that parallel human disease progression.
